claim_chain: Power outages are increasing in frequency and duration with climate change
Prior studies from New York State show associations between outages and hospitalizations but nationwide data were limited
We developed a national dataset and found power outages were associated with increased CVD and respiratory hospitalizations
Larger outages had stronger associations with hospitalization rates
The lagged effects differ between CVD and respiratory hospitalizations
Improving electricity reliability could support community health and protect older adults

typical_pattern: State main finding with quantitative result
Compare to prior literature and contextualize
State implications for policy or future research

likely_priorities: Exposure measurement accuracy and spatial resolution
Control for confounding by extreme weather events
Competing risk of mortality versus hospitalization
likely_attacks: County-level exposure may introduce misclassification bias
Cannot directly assess co-exposure to extreme weather
DME use quartiles based on full Medicare population but study uses Fee-For-Service only
No individual-level DME use data to test mortality as competing risk
Protective effect at 4-5 days may be artifact of spline model
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly states limitations including: county-level power outage exposure with no finer resolution data available nationally; inability to directly assess co-exposure to extreme weather; DME quartiles based on full Medicare population while study uses Fee-For-Service beneficiaries; lack of individual-level DME data; uncertainty about whether protective effect at 4-5 days is harvesting effect or model artifact; and exposure misclassification concerns for smaller outages.

examples.quoted_phrase: we found that power outages were associated with increased acute emergency CVD and respiratory hospitalizations
Power outages may increase the risk of CVD and respiratory hospitalizations
may be a harvesting effect or an artifact resulting from the use of a natural spline-based model
making it unclear whether any true protective effect exists
Power outages may lead to CVD and respiratory hospitalizations in older adults due to increased heat exposure, cold exposure, stress, and loss of electricity

phrase_evidence.phrase: In this 2018 case-crossover study of over 23 million Fee-For-Service Medicare beneficiaries aged 65+
we found that power outages were associated with increased acute emergency CVD and respiratory hospitalizations
This association was strongest for CVD hospitalizations the day following the power outage
while for respiratory hospitalizations, the same-day association was strongest
As expected, larger outages affecting ≥3% or ≥5% versus ≥1% of county customers had stronger associations
With outage frequency and duration increasing due to climate change
these outages may pose a growing threat to the cardiovascular and respiratory health of older adults
Several New York State-based studies have reported associations between power outage exposure and increased CVD and respiratory acute care visits
We observed a protective effect on hospitalizations 4–5 days after a power outage, which may be a harvesting effect or an artifact
making it unclear whether any true protective effect exists
We hypothesized that power outages may lead to CVD and respiratory hospitalizations in older adults due to increased heat exposure, cold exposure, stress, and loss of electricity
The lagged effects of CVD and respiratory hospitalizations due to power outage likely differ
many power outages are caused by climate-related severe weather like heat waves, winter storms, hurricanes, wildfires, and floods
which likely amplifies health risks
While we did not directly assess co-exposure to extreme weather in this study
we controlled for temperature, wind, and precipitation as time-varying confounders
Enabled by new national datasets of power outage exposure
further research could better describe the lagged effects
The reason may be 2-fold
We expect less exposure misclassification when ≥5% of county customers are without power compared to ≥1% of customers
Any resulting bias, therefore, would be towards the null
the duration of power outage that impacts health remains unknown
We did not find evidence for a threshold
We found that even one-hour outages were associated with increased cardiovascular and respiratory hospitalizations
suggesting that even short outages can have substantial population health impacts
Contrary to our hypotheses, we did not observe effect modification by age, sex, or Medicaid eligibility
We hypothesized the opposite
Several factors may explain these unexpected findings
Our study had several limitations
Improving electricity reliability could support community health and protect older adults

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included. The profile accurately reflects the paper's epidemiological focus, moderate hedging, and balanced claim strength with explicit limitations discussion.
